subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Succession Law: Testamentary Capacity, Formalities, Construction, Legal Right Share, Section 117, and Intestacy

questions.question: Under Banks v Goodfellow [1870], which of the following correctly states the FIRST element of testamentary capacity?

options.A: The testator must understand the nature of making a will and its effects
options.B: The testator must understand the extent of the property they are disposing of
options.C: The testator must understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit
options.D: The testator must not be suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons their affections

questions.explanation: The first element of Banks v Goodfellow [1870] requires the testator to understand the nature of making a will and its effects — they must appreciate what a will is and what executing it means for the disposition of their property on death.

questions.question: Under Banks v Goodfellow [1870], which of the following correctly states the SECOND element of testamentary capacity?

options.A: The testator must understand the nature of making a will and its effects
options.B: The testator must understand the extent of the property they are disposing of
options.C: The testator must understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit
options.D: The testator must not be suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons their affections

questions.explanation: The second element of Banks v Goodfellow [1870] requires the testator to understand the extent of the property they are disposing of. They need not know the precise value of every asset, but must have a general appreciation of what they own and what is being disposed of.

questions.question: Under Banks v Goodfellow [1870], which of the following correctly states the THIRD element of testamentary capacity?

options.A: The testator must understand the nature of making a will and its effects
options.B: The testator must understand the extent of the property they are disposing of
options.C: The testator must understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit, even if they choose to exclude them
options.D: The testator must not be suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons their affections

questions.explanation: The third element requires the testator to understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit from the estate — such as close family members — even if the testator ultimately chooses to exclude them. This ensures the testator is making an informed and deliberate choice in departing from expected distributions.

questions.question: Under Banks v Goodfellow [1870], which of the following correctly states the FOURTH element of testamentary capacity?

options.A: The testator must not be suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons their affections or perverts their sense of right
options.B: The testator must understand the nature of making a will and its effects
options.C: The testator must understand the extent of the property they are disposing of
options.D: The testator must understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit

questions.explanation: The fourth element of Banks v Goodfellow [1870] requires that the testator not be suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons their affections, perverts their sense of right, or prevents the exercise of their natural faculties. This addresses conditions such as insane delusions that could distort the testamentary act even where the other three elements are met.

questions.question: How many elements does the Banks v Goodfellow [1870] test for testamentary capacity comprise?

options.A: Two
options.B: Four
options.C: Five
options.D: Three

questions.explanation: The Banks v Goodfellow [1870] test comprises four elements: (1) understanding the nature of a will and its effects; (2) understanding the extent of the property being disposed of; (3) understanding the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit; and (4) not suffering from a disorder of the mind that poisons affections or perverts the sense of right.

questions.question: Under section 77 of the Succession Act 1965, what is the statutory requirement for testamentary capacity?

options.A: The testator must be of full age and of sound body
options.B: The testator must not be subject to undue influence at the time of execution
options.C: The testator must be of 'sound disposing mind' at the time of execution
options.D: The testator must have read and understood the full contents of the will before signing

questions.explanation: Section 77 of the Succession Act 1965 requires the testator to be of 'sound disposing mind' at the time of execution. This statutory formulation incorporates the common law testamentary capacity test from Banks v Goodfellow [1870] and confirms that capacity must be present at the moment the will is formally executed.

questions.question: Re Coughlan's Estate [2022] confirmed that testamentary capacity is assessed at which point in time?

options.A: The date of execution of the will
options.B: The date the will is lodged for probate
options.C: The date instructions are given to the solicitor drafting the will
options.D: The date of the testator's death

questions.explanation: Re Coughlan's Estate [2022] confirmed that testamentary capacity is assessed at the date of execution of the will. A testator may have lost capacity between giving instructions and executing the will, or may have regained capacity during a lucid interval. The critical date is the moment of formal execution under section 77 of the Succession Act 1965.

questions.question: Which statement best reflects the position in Re Coughlan's Estate [2022] regarding persons with cognitive decline?

options.A: A person with dementia can never make a valid will, regardless of their apparent condition on the day of execution
options.B: A person with significant cognitive decline may have lucid intervals during which a valid will can be made
options.C: Evidence of cognitive decline raises a conclusive presumption of incapacity
options.D: The court must appoint a medical examiner before any will by an older testator can be admitted to probate

questions.explanation: Re Coughlan's Estate [2022] confirmed that a person with significant cognitive decline may have lucid intervals — periods of sufficient clarity — during which they possess testamentary capacity and can execute a valid will. A diagnosis of dementia does not automatically invalidate a will; capacity must be assessed on the actual facts as they stood at the date of execution.

questions.question: Where a will is challenged on grounds of lack of testamentary capacity, who bears the burden of proving incapacity?

options.A: The personal representatives of the deceased
options.B: The testator's solicitor who supervised execution of the will
options.C: The party challenging the will
options.D: The beneficiaries named in the will

questions.explanation: As confirmed in Re Coughlan's Estate [2022], the burden of proving incapacity lies with the party challenging the will. There is a presumption in favour of capacity — particularly where the will was executed in a formally regular manner — and the challenger must adduce sufficient evidence to displace that presumption on the balance of probabilities.

questions.question: Which of the following is a requirement under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 for a valid will?

options.A: The will must be in writing
options.B: The will must be typed and printed — a handwritten will is not valid
options.C: The will must be witnessed by a solicitor or notary public
options.D: The will must include a declaration by the testator of their mental capacity

questions.explanation: Section 78(1) of the Succession Act 1965 requires that a will be in writing. This is the first formal requirement. The section imposes no requirement as to a particular form of writing — a handwritten will is valid. Witnesses are not required to be legal professionals, and no capacity declaration is required on the face of the document.

questions.question: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, where must the testator sign the will?

options.A: At the top of the first page, to confirm their identity
options.B: At the foot or end of the will
options.C: On every page, to prevent subsequent tampering
options.D: In the margin alongside the attestation clause

questions.explanation: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 requires the testator to sign the will at the foot or end thereof. This requirement ensures the testator has read and adopted everything appearing above their signature. A signature placed elsewhere — such as at the top of the document — may render the will invalid as not complying with section 78.

questions.question: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, what is required regarding the presence of witnesses at the time the testator signs?

options.A: The witnesses may sign at different times, provided both sign within one week of the testator
options.B: Only one witness need be present when the testator signs; the second may sign the will later
options.C: Both witnesses must be present at the same time when the testator makes or acknowledges their signature
options.D: Witnesses need not be present together and may each sign in the presence of a solicitor

questions.explanation: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 requires that the testator's signature be made or acknowledged in the presence of two or more witnesses present at the same time. Both witnesses must be physically present simultaneously when the testator signs or acknowledges the signature. Sequential witnessing — one witness present at a time — does not satisfy this statutory requirement.

questions.question: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, where must each witness sign the will?

options.A: In the presence of the testator
options.B: In the presence of both the other witness and the testator together
options.C: In the presence of a Commissioner for Oaths or a Peace Commissioner
options.D: At any location, provided they sign within seven days of the testator's execution

questions.explanation: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 requires that each witness sign in the presence of the testator. The witnesses do not need to sign in each other's presence — only in the presence of the testator. A witness who leaves the room before signing fails to satisfy this requirement, potentially invalidating the execution.

questions.question: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, what is the consequence where a beneficiary under the will witnesses the will?

options.A: The will is entirely void and cannot be admitted to probate
options.B: The will remains valid but the gift to the beneficiary-witness is void
options.C: The beneficiary forfeits all claims under both the will and intestacy
options.D: The attestation is invalid and the entire execution is treated as defective

questions.explanation: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, if a beneficiary witnesses the will, the will itself remains valid and can be admitted to probate — but the gift to that beneficiary-witness is void. The will is not invalidated; only the specific gift to the offending witness is struck out. This rule incentivises the use of independent witnesses.

questions.question: What is the position under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 if the spouse of a beneficiary witnesses the will?

options.A: The will is void for non-compliance with the attestation requirements
options.B: The gift to the beneficiary is voidable at the court's discretion upon application
options.C: The will remains valid but the gift to that beneficiary is void
options.D: The gift to the beneficiary remains valid unless the court orders otherwise

questions.explanation: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 extends the no-benefit rule to the spouse of a beneficiary. If the spouse of a beneficiary witnesses the will, the will itself remains valid but the gift to that beneficiary is void. This prevents indirect benefit flowing to the beneficiary through the witnessing spouse, closing an obvious avenue for abuse.

questions.question: When construing a will, what is the starting point for the court under the four corners rule?

options.A: The court looks at the document itself and gives the words their natural, ordinary meaning
options.B: The court considers the testator's oral instructions given to their solicitor at the time of drafting
options.C: The court examines all surrounding circumstances of the testator's life before turning to the text
options.D: The court applies the contra proferentem principle against the draftsperson of the will

questions.explanation: The four corners rule provides that when construing a will, the court begins by looking within the four corners of the document itself, giving the words used their natural and ordinary meaning. Only if ambiguity persists after this exercise does the court move to supplementary aids of construction such as the armchair principle or extrinsic evidence under section 90.

questions.question: What is the armchair principle in will construction?

options.A: The court disregards all extrinsic evidence and interprets the will in complete isolation from surrounding circumstances
options.B: The court places itself in the position of the testator at the time the will was made, to understand the context in which the words were used
options.C: The court applies a strict literalist approach, refusing to consider the testator's subjective intentions
options.D: The court gives effect to the most recent version of any clause where the will contains internal inconsistencies

questions.explanation: The armchair principle requires the court to place itself metaphorically in the armchair of the testator — to consider the surrounding circumstances as they existed at the time the will was made — so as to understand the context in which particular words were used. It is applied after the four corners rule fails to resolve ambiguity, before recourse to section 90 extrinsic evidence.

questions.question: Under section 90 of the Succession Act 1965, in what circumstances is extrinsic evidence admissible in will construction?

options.A: In all cases of ambiguity, both patent and latent, as well as where the will is clear on its face
options.B: Only where a named beneficiary has predeceased the testator and the gift must be redirected
options.C: To resolve a latent ambiguity, including evidence of the testator's intentions
options.D: Only where the will was typewritten and the testator signed in pencil rather than ink

questions.explanation: Section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 permits extrinsic evidence — including evidence of the testator's intentions — to be admitted to resolve a latent ambiguity. A latent ambiguity arises where the will appears clear on its face but becomes ambiguous when applied to the surrounding facts (e.g., two people or properties matching the description used in the will). Rowe v Law [1978] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on section 90.

questions.question: Rowe v Law [1978] is significant in Irish succession law because it is:

options.A: The leading Irish Supreme Court case on section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 and the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in will construction
options.B: The leading Irish authority establishing the Banks v Goodfellow testamentary capacity test in domestic law
options.C: The leading Irish case on the priority of the legal right share over testamentary dispositions
options.D: The first Irish case to apply the section 117 moral duty test to the child of a wealthy estate

questions.explanation: Rowe v Law [1978] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 and the admissibility of extrinsic evidence to resolve latent ambiguity in wills. FE-1 candidates answering questions on will construction and section 90 are expected to cite Rowe v Law [1978] as the primary Irish authority.

questions.question: Under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965, what is the legal right share of a surviving spouse where the deceased dies testate with no children?

options.A: One quarter of the estate
options.B: One half of the estate
options.C: Two thirds of the estate
options.D: One third of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 111(1) of the Succession Act 1965, where the testator leaves a spouse but no children, the spouse's legal right share is one half of the estate. This entitlement arises regardless of the contents of the will and takes priority over all testamentary dispositions.

questions.question: Under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965, what is the legal right share of a surviving spouse where the deceased dies testate and is survived by children?

options.A: One quarter of the estate
options.B: One half of the estate
options.C: One third of the estate
options.D: Two thirds of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 111(2) of the Succession Act 1965, where the testator leaves both a spouse and children, the spouse's legal right share is one third of the estate. The presence of children reduces the spouse's share from one half to one third, reflecting the competing claims of the children on the estate.

questions.question: What priority does the legal right share under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965 have in relation to testamentary dispositions?

options.A: The legal right share takes priority over all testamentary dispositions
options.B: The legal right share ranks equally with testamentary dispositions made to children
options.C: The legal right share is subject to the satisfaction of specific legacies before it can be claimed
options.D: The legal right share ranks behind gifts to registered charities made under the will

questions.explanation: Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965 expressly provides that the spouse's legal right share takes priority over all testamentary dispositions. The spouse's entitlement must therefore be satisfied before any gifts under the will are distributed to other beneficiaries — including children, other relatives, and charities.

questions.question: Under section 115 of the Succession Act 1965, what right does a surviving spouse have where the will gives them less than their legal right share?

options.A: The spouse may challenge the validity of the will on grounds of fraud or undue influence
options.B: The spouse may elect to take the legal right share instead of the gift under the will
options.C: The spouse automatically receives whichever is greater — the testamentary gift or the legal right share
options.D: The spouse must apply to the Circuit Court within six months for a property adjustment order

questions.explanation: Under section 115 of the Succession Act 1965, if the gift to the spouse under the will is less than their legal right share, the spouse may elect to take the legal right share instead of the testamentary gift. The election must be actively exercised by the spouse — the legal right share does not automatically displace the smaller testamentary gift.

questions.question: The right of election under section 115 of the Succession Act 1965 is best described as:

options.A: A mandatory requirement that the surviving spouse must exercise within three months of probate being granted
options.B: A right that arises automatically without any positive act required from the surviving spouse
options.C: A choice available to the surviving spouse between accepting the testamentary gift or taking the legal right share
options.D: A right exercisable by the children to require the surviving spouse to waive the legal right share

questions.explanation: Section 115 of the Succession Act 1965 gives the surviving spouse the choice — or election — between taking the testamentary gift under the will or taking their legal right share under section 111. It is the spouse's personal choice, must be actively exercised, and does not arise automatically. The time limit and procedure for election are governed by section 115.

questions.question: Under section 117 of the Succession Act 1965, what must be established before the court can order provision from the estate for a child?

options.A: The testator failed in their moral duty to make proper provision for the child in accordance with their means
options.B: The child was financially dependent on the testator at the date of the testator's death
options.C: The testator expressly excluded the child from the will without providing any written explanation
options.D: The child was a minor at the date of the testator's death

questions.explanation: Section 117(1) of the Succession Act 1965 provides that where the court is satisfied that a testator has failed in their moral duty to make proper provision for a child in accordance with their means, the court may order that such provision be made from the estate. The threshold is moral duty — not financial dependence, not explicit exclusion, and not the child's age.

questions.question: What key principle was confirmed in In Re AC [2005] in relation to section 117 of the Succession Act 1965?

options.A: A testator with substantial assets must make equal provision for all children in their will
options.B: The testator retains the right to distribute their estate freely — section 117 is a floor, not a formula
options.C: Section 117 creates an absolute entitlement to provision, overriding testamentary freedom in all cases
options.D: The court must apply a mathematical formula based on the child's needs relative to the estate

questions.explanation: In Re AC [2005] confirmed that a testator retains the right to distribute their estate as they see fit — section 117 does not remove testamentary freedom. Section 117 establishes a floor: a minimum moral obligation. It is not a formula entitling every child to a proportionate share. The court assesses whether the testator discharged their moral duty in the particular circumstances of the case.

questions.question: Within what period must a section 117 application be brought under the Succession Act 1965?

options.A: Six months from the date of the testator's death
options.B: Two years from the date of the first grant of representation
options.C: Twelve months from the first grant of representation
options.D: Three months from the date probate is extracted

questions.explanation: Section 117(6) of the Succession Act 1965 requires that an application under section 117 be made within twelve months from the first taking out of representation of the deceased's estate. This is a strict limitation period — failure to apply within twelve months will bar the claim entirely.

questions.question: In assessing whether a testator discharged their moral duty under section 117, what does the court consider?

options.A: All provision made by the testator for the child during their lifetime, as well as the contents of the will
options.B: Only the contents of the will, without regard to provision made during the testator's lifetime
options.C: The child's earning capacity and financial independence at the date of the application
options.D: The size of the estate relative to the number of children who survived the testator

questions.explanation: Under section 117, the court considers all provision made by the testator for the child — both during the testator's lifetime and by way of the will. A child who received substantial gifts, educational funding, or other support during the testator's lifetime may find it harder to establish a failure of moral duty, even where nothing is left in the will.

questions.question: Under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965, where a person dies intestate survived by a spouse but no children, what does the spouse receive?

options.A: Two thirds of the estate
options.B: The entire estate
options.C: Three quarters of the estate
options.D: One half of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 67(1) of the Succession Act 1965, where a person dies intestate and is survived by a spouse but no children, the spouse takes the entire estate. There are no children to share with, and the surviving spouse inherits everything on intestacy.

questions.question: Under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965, where a person dies intestate survived by both a spouse and children, what share does the spouse receive?

options.A: One half of the estate
options.B: Three quarters of the estate
options.C: Two thirds of the estate
options.D: One third of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 67(2) of the Succession Act 1965, where the intestate is survived by both a spouse and children, the spouse takes two thirds of the estate. The remaining one third is divided equally among the children. This reflects a balance between the rights of the surviving spouse and the interests of the children.

questions.question: Under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965, where a person dies intestate survived by a spouse and children, how is the children's portion distributed?

options.A: The children share one third equally among themselves
options.B: The children share one half equally among themselves
options.C: The children take two thirds and the spouse takes one third
options.D: The eldest child takes a double share with the remainder divided equally among the others

questions.explanation: Under section 67(2) of the Succession Act 1965, the one-third children's share is divided equally among all children. Each child takes an equal portion — there is no preference for an eldest or firstborn child. Where a child has predeceased but left their own children, those grandchildren take their parent's share by representation.

questions.question: Under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965, where a person dies intestate with no surviving spouse, how is the estate distributed?

options.A: The estate passes to the State as bona vacantia
options.B: The entire estate passes to the children equally
options.C: Half passes to the children and the other half to the testator's parents
options.D: The estate is divided equally among all surviving relatives up to the fourth degree

questions.explanation: Under section 67(3) of the Succession Act 1965, where there is no surviving spouse, the entire estate passes to the children equally. Each child takes an equal share. The estate does not pass to the State or to the deceased's parents while children survive.

questions.question: A testator executed a will leaving their entire estate to a charity, excluding their adult children. The adult children challenge the will, claiming the testator lacked capacity at execution. Which authority governs the test for testamentary capacity?

options.A: Re Coughlan's Estate [2022], which provides that capacity is assessed at the date probate is extracted
options.B: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965, which creates a moral duty test applicable to children
options.C: Banks v Goodfellow [1870], which sets out the four-element testamentary capacity test
options.D: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, which governs the formal validity of wills

questions.explanation: The test for testamentary capacity in Irish law is drawn from Banks v Goodfellow [1870], incorporated into section 77 of the Succession Act 1965. Banks v Goodfellow sets out the four elements that must be satisfied. Re Coughlan's Estate [2022] is relevant to the date of assessment and burden of proof; section 78 governs formalities; section 117 concerns the separate question of moral duty.

questions.question: Aoife makes a will leaving her house to her daughter Brigid. Brigid's husband Conor witnesses the will. Which of the following best states the legal consequences under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965?

options.A: The will remains valid but the gift of the house to Brigid is void, as Conor is the spouse of a beneficiary
options.B: The will is entirely void because the attestation requirements have not been complied with
options.C: The will is voidable at the court's discretion, and Brigid may apply to the court to save the gift
options.D: The gift to Brigid is valid but Conor's evidence as to the circumstances of execution is inadmissible

questions.explanation: Under section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, where the spouse of a beneficiary witnesses the will, the will itself remains valid and admissible to probate, but the gift to that beneficiary is void. Conor is the spouse of Brigid (the beneficiary), so the gift of the house to Brigid fails. The will is not entirely invalidated — only the specific gift to Brigid is void.

questions.question: A testator's will leaves 'my fishing cottage in the west' to their nephew. There are two properties matching that description. After applying the four corners rule without resolution, which principle should the court apply next?

options.A: Section 90 of the Succession Act 1965, immediately admitting all available extrinsic evidence
options.B: The armchair principle — the court places itself in the position of the testator to understand the context in which the words were used
options.C: The contra proferentem rule, construing the ambiguity strictly against the draftsperson of the will
options.D: The four corners rule afresh, re-reading the document for any overlooked plain meaning

questions.explanation: After the four corners rule fails to resolve the ambiguity, the court applies the armchair principle — considering the surrounding circumstances as known to the testator at the time the will was made. If ambiguity persists after the armchair principle, section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 permits extrinsic evidence to resolve the latent ambiguity. Rowe v Law [1978] is the leading Irish authority on section 90.

questions.question: David dies testate. His estate is worth €600,000. He is survived by his wife Eleanor and two children. His will leaves Eleanor €150,000 and divides the remainder equally between the two children. What is Eleanor's legal right share under section 111?

options.A: €300,000, being one half of the estate
options.B: €150,000, being one quarter of the estate
options.C: €200,000, being one third of the estate as children survive
options.D: €400,000, being two thirds of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 111(2) of the Succession Act 1965, where the deceased leaves a spouse and children, the spouse's legal right share is one third. One third of €600,000 is €200,000. Eleanor's legal right share is therefore €200,000 — exceeding the €150,000 testamentary gift. Eleanor may elect under section 115 to take the higher legal right share.

questions.question: Eleanor's gift under David's will is €150,000 but her legal right share under section 111 is €200,000. What option is available to Eleanor under section 115?

options.A: Eleanor may elect to take her legal right share of €200,000 instead of the testamentary gift of €150,000
options.B: Eleanor must accept the €150,000 gift as she cannot challenge a valid testamentary disposition
options.C: Eleanor may bring a section 117 application to supplement the gift under the will
options.D: Eleanor must apply to the High Court within three months to enforce her legal right share

questions.explanation: Section 115 of the Succession Act 1965 entitles Eleanor to elect to take her legal right share of €200,000 instead of the €150,000 gift under the will. As the legal right share exceeds the testamentary gift, it is in Eleanor's interest to elect. Section 117 is a remedy for children, not spouses. There is no three-month High Court deadline for electing under section 115.

questions.question: Frank dies, leaving his entire estate to his second wife. His son George, aged 35 and financially independent, received no provision in the will or during Frank's lifetime, despite Frank having a substantial estate. George wishes to bring a section 117 application. Which of the following is correct?

options.A: George cannot bring a section 117 application as he is over 18 and financially independent
options.B: George may bring a section 117 application, which must be made within twelve months of the first grant of representation
options.C: George's application will automatically succeed as he received no provision at all during Frank's lifetime
options.D: George must first establish that the will was procured by the undue influence of Frank's second wife

questions.explanation: Section 117 is not limited to minor or dependent children — adult children may apply. George may bring a section 117 application, provided he does so within twelve months of the first grant of representation. The application will not automatically succeed: the court will assess whether Frank failed in his moral duty, considering all circumstances including George's financial independence (In Re AC [2005]).

questions.question: Hannah dies intestate. She is survived by her husband Ian and their three children. Her estate is worth €900,000. What does Ian receive under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965?

options.A: €450,000 — one half of the estate
options.B: €225,000 — one quarter of the estate
options.C: €900,000 — the entire estate as surviving spouse
options.D: €600,000 — two thirds of the estate

questions.explanation: Under section 67(2) of the Succession Act 1965, where the intestate is survived by both a spouse and children, the spouse takes two thirds. Two thirds of €900,000 is €600,000. Ian therefore receives €600,000. The remaining €300,000 (one third) is divided equally among the three children — €100,000 each.

questions.question: Continuing from the previous question — Hannah's estate is €900,000; Ian receives €600,000 (two thirds). How is the one-third children's share distributed among the three children?

options.A: The three children share two thirds (€600,000) equally at €200,000 each
options.B: The eldest child receives a double share and the remaining two divide the balance
options.C: The children take nothing — their entitlement vests only if the surviving spouse predeceases them
options.D: The three children share one third (€300,000) equally at €100,000 each

questions.explanation: Under section 67(2) of the Succession Act 1965, the one-third children's share (€300,000) is divided equally among all three children — €100,000 each. There is no priority for the eldest child, and the children's entitlement arises immediately upon the intestate's death, not contingent upon the subsequent death of the surviving spouse.

questions.question: Karl was diagnosed with moderate dementia three years before his death. Two months before he died, during a period of apparent clarity, he executed a will in the presence of two independent witnesses. The will was challenged on grounds of incapacity. Which principle applies?

options.A: The will is automatically void because Karl had been diagnosed with dementia prior to execution
options.B: The will is voidable — a presumption of incapacity arises automatically once dementia is established
options.C: The court assesses capacity at the time instructions were given to the solicitor, not at the date of execution
options.D: A person with cognitive decline may have lucid intervals during which a valid will can be made; capacity is assessed at the date of execution

questions.explanation: As confirmed in Re Coughlan's Estate [2022], a prior diagnosis of dementia does not automatically deprive a person of testamentary capacity. A testator may have lucid intervals — periods of sufficient clarity — during which a valid will can be executed. Capacity is assessed at the date of execution, and the burden of proving incapacity lies with the party challenging the will.

questions.question: Linda's will is challenged by her estranged son Michael on grounds that she lacked testamentary capacity when she executed it. Who bears the burden of proving incapacity?

options.A: Linda's personal representatives, as they are the parties seeking to give effect to the will
options.B: The beneficiaries named in the will, who have an interest in upholding it
options.C: The burden is shared equally between the challenger and the beneficiaries on the balance of probabilities
options.D: Michael, as the party challenging the will, bears the burden of proving incapacity

questions.explanation: As confirmed in Re Coughlan's Estate [2022], the burden of proving incapacity lies with the party challenging the will — here, Michael. There is a presumption in favour of capacity, particularly where the will was executed in an apparently regular and formal manner. Michael must produce sufficient evidence to displace that presumption.

questions.question: Nina's will refers to 'my red car.' At the date of her death, Nina owned two red cars. The court has examined the will itself and cannot resolve the ambiguity. Which provision governs the admissibility of evidence to resolve this?

options.A: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965, which governs the formal validity requirements for wills
options.B: Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965, which governs the legal right share of a surviving spouse
options.C: Section 67 of the Succession Act 1965, which provides the intestacy distribution rules
options.D: Section 90 of the Succession Act 1965, which permits extrinsic evidence to resolve a latent ambiguity

questions.explanation: This is a latent ambiguity: the will appears clear on its face ('my red car'), but becomes ambiguous when applied to the facts (two red cars). Section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 permits extrinsic evidence — including evidence of the testator's intentions — to be admitted to resolve such a latent ambiguity. Rowe v Law [1978] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on section 90.

questions.question: In an FE-1 examination question on will construction and section 90 of the Succession Act 1965, which case is the most important Irish authority to cite?

options.A: Banks v Goodfellow [1870]
options.B: Re Coughlan's Estate [2022]
options.C: In Re AC [2005]
options.D: Rowe v Law [1978]

questions.explanation: Rowe v Law [1978] is the leading Irish Supreme Court case on section 90 of the Succession Act 1965 and is the key authority to cite in FE-1 answers on will construction and the admission of extrinsic evidence to resolve latent ambiguity. Banks v Goodfellow governs capacity; Re Coughlan's Estate concerns capacity and burden of proof; In Re AC concerns section 117 moral duty.

questions.question: Rose wishes to bring a section 117 application in respect of her late father's estate. The first grant of representation was taken out on 1 January 2024. By what date must Rose's application be made at the latest?

options.A: 1 July 2024, being six months from the first grant of representation
options.B: 1 January 2026, being two years from the first grant of representation
options.C: 1 January 2024, being the date of the first grant of representation
options.D: 1 January 2025, being twelve months from the first grant of representation

questions.explanation: Section 117(6) of the Succession Act 1965 requires that a section 117 application be made within twelve months of the first taking out of representation of the deceased's estate. Where representation was first granted on 1 January 2024, the twelve-month period expires on 1 January 2025. A failure to apply within this strict period bars the claim.

questions.question: Under section 117 of the Succession Act 1965, as interpreted in In Re AC [2005], which of the following statements is INCORRECT?

options.A: The court considers all provision made by the testator during their lifetime, not only the contents of the will
options.B: The test under section 117 is based on moral duty rather than any mathematical entitlement
options.C: The testator retains the right to distribute their estate freely
options.D: Section 117 guarantees every child a minimum fixed share of the estate regardless of the circumstances

questions.explanation: Statement D is incorrect. Section 117 does not guarantee every child a minimum fixed share — it is a moral duty test, not an entitlement formula. As confirmed in In Re AC [2005], section 117 is a floor, not a formula, and the testator retains the freedom to distribute their estate. Whether a moral duty exists depends on the specific circumstances of each case.

questions.question: Sean dies testate, leaving his entire estate to his favourite nephew. His wife Theresa has not been mentioned in the will at all. What is Theresa's legal position?

options.A: Theresa has no claim on the estate as she was not included in the will and cannot challenge a valid testamentary disposition
options.B: Theresa can claim only if she can prove the will was obtained by undue influence of the nephew
options.C: Theresa is entitled to a half share on intestacy because the will failed to provide for her
options.D: Theresa is entitled to her legal right share under section 111, which takes priority over the testamentary disposition to the nephew

questions.explanation: Theresa is entitled to her legal right share under section 111 of the Succession Act 1965, regardless of what the will says. As surviving spouse with no children, she is entitled to one half of the estate; if children survive, she is entitled to one third. The legal right share takes priority over all testamentary dispositions, including the gift to the nephew. Theresa may elect under section 115 to take her legal right share.

questions.question: Una dies intestate. She has no surviving spouse. She is survived by four children. How is her estate distributed under section 67 of the Succession Act 1965?

options.A: The estate is divided equally among the children and Una's surviving parents
options.B: The eldest child takes a double share; the remaining three children share the balance equally
options.C: Half the estate passes to the children and the other half to Una's nearest next of kin
options.D: The entire estate is divided equally among the four children

questions.explanation: Under section 67(3) of the Succession Act 1965, where there is no surviving spouse, the entire estate passes to the children equally. All four children take equal shares. There is no preference for an eldest child, no entitlement for parents or other relatives while children survive, and no division with other next of kin.

questions.question: Victor executes a will in writing and signs it at the foot. Two witnesses are present simultaneously when Victor acknowledges his signature. One witness then signs in Victor's presence; the second leaves the room before signing the will. Which of the following is correct?

options.A: The will is fully valid — it is sufficient that both witnesses were present when Victor acknowledged his signature
options.B: The will is valid but the second witness's attestation is voidable at the court's discretion
options.C: Victor must re-execute the will with both witnesses present simultaneously to cure the defect
options.D: The will does not comply with section 78, as each witness must sign in the presence of the testator

questions.explanation: Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965 requires each witness to sign in the presence of the testator. Where the second witness leaves the room before signing, they do not sign in Victor's presence — this breaches the formal requirements of section 78. The will may not be admitted to probate as validly executed.

questions.question: An FE-1 candidate is asked to advise on the estate of a person who died testate, survived by a spouse and two adult children. The will leaves the entire estate to a charity. Which answer provides the most complete and accurate advice?

options.A: The will is fully effective — testamentary freedom is absolute in Irish law and the charity receives the entire estate
options.B: The spouse may challenge the will on the basis that it was not made in contemplation of the marriage
options.C: The children have an automatic right to equal shares by virtue of section 67 of the Succession Act 1965
options.D: The spouse is entitled to a legal right share of one third under section 111; the children may each bring a section 117 application within twelve months of the first grant of representation if the testator failed in their moral duty

questions.explanation: D is the most complete and accurate advice. The surviving spouse is entitled to a legal right share of one third (children survive) under section 111, taking priority over the testamentary gift to the charity. The adult children may each bring a section 117 application within twelve months of the first grant of representation, if the testator failed in their moral duty to provide for them (In Re AC [2005]). Section 67 applies on intestacy, not to a testate estate.
